title: Whakairo Taiao: Carving Our River's Story / Te Whakairo i te Kōrero o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural and environmental significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a vital waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native falcon (kārearea) that once hunted along its banks, and its waters hold stories of ancestral journeys, mahinga kai (traditional food gathering), and environmental changes. Students will connect with local kaumātua and artists to understand how the awa has been represented in traditional and contemporary Māori art forms.
learningObjectives: Create a mixed-media artwork that visually interprets a specific narrative or environmental issue related to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, demonstrating understanding of at least two artistic techniques.
Analyze and explain how at least one traditional Māori art form (e.g., whakairo, kowhaiwhai) communicates cultural values and knowledge about the local environment.
Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for a public art installation that responds to an environmental challenge facing the awa, incorporating community and mātauranga Māori perspectives.

activities.title: He Haerenga: Journey to the Awa
activities.description: A guided field trip to significant sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Led by a local kaumātua, students will hear pūrākau (stories) of the awa, learn its original and colonial names, and identify native flora and fauna. Students will use sketchbooks and digital cameras to document textures, patterns, colors, and forms inspired by the environment for use in their artwork.

activities.title: Ngā Toi Taketake: Exploring Traditional Art Forms
activities.description: A workshop with a local Māori artist or carver. Students will learn the basic elements and meanings behind traditional art forms such as the koru and pikorua patterns found in kowhaiwhai (rafter patterns) and whakairo (carving), which often depict stories of creation, genealogy, and the natural world. They will practice sketching these patterns and discuss how they could be adapted to tell a modern story of the awa.

activities.title: Te Auahatanga: Creative Response Studio
activities.description: In the art studio, students will use their field notes, sketches, and photographic evidence to plan and create a mixed-media artwork. This could be a painting, a collage, a digital print, or a small-scale sculpture. The artwork must visually communicate a personal response to the awa, incorporating either a learned traditional pattern or a self-developed pattern inspired by the stream's ecosystem.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Ā-Iwi: Community Art Proposal
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will role-play as artists commissioned by the local council. They will develop a concept for a public art installation (e.g., a mural, a sculpture garden, an interactive sound piece) designed to raise awareness about a specific issue affecting the awa, such as pollution or habitat loss. Their proposal must explain how the artwork incorporates mātauranga Māori and engages the community.

resources: Local kaumātua and/or a representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi authority for cultural guidance and storytelling.
Local Māori artist or carver for a practical workshop.
Digital cameras or tablets for environmental documentation.
NZ History website: 'The Mangakōtukutuku dispute' article for historical context on land and water issues.

tasks: Submission of a final mixed-media artwork with an artist's statement.
A 3-minute individual presentation explaining the cultural and environmental concepts behind their artwork.
A group-written one-page proposal for a public art installation.

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the characteristics, uses, and purposes of objects and images from past and present cultures, identifying the roles of artists and their work in communities (Level 4).
Developing Practical Knowledge: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and selected principles through the use of materials and processes (Level 4).
Developing Ideas: Develop and revisit visual ideas, in response to a variety of motivations, observation, and imagination, supported by the study of artists' works (Level 4).
Communicating and Interpreting: Explore and describe ways in which meanings can be communicated and interpreted in their own and others' work (Level 4).
